overview: (a) The National Curriculum Statement Grades R-12 (January 2012) represents a policy statement for learning and teaching in South African schools and comprises the following: (i) Curriculum and Assessment Policy Statements for each approved school subject; (ii) The policy document, National policy pertaining to the programme and promotion requirements of the National Curriculum Statement Grades R-12; and (iii) (b) The policy document, National Protocol for Assessment Grades R-12 (January 2012). The National Curriculum Statement Grades R-12 (January 2012) replaces the two current national curricula statements, namely the (i) Revised National Curriculum Statement Grades R-9, Government Gazette No. 23406 of 31 May 2002, and (ii) National Curriculum Statement Grades 10-12 Government Gazettes, No. 25545 of 6 October 2003 and No. 27594 of 17 May 2005. (c) The national curriculum statements contemplated in subparagraphs b(i) and (ii) comprise the following policy documents which will be incrementally repealed by the National Curriculum Statement Grades R-12 (January 2012) during the period 2012-2014: (i) The Learning Area/Subject Statements, Learning Programme Guidelines and Subject Assessment Guidelines for Grades R-9 and Grades 10-12; (ii) The policy document, National Policy on assessment and qualifications for schools in the General Education and Training Band, promulgated in Government Notice No. 124 in Government Gazette No. 29626 of 12 February 2007; (iii) The policy document, the National Senior Certificate: A qualification at Level 4 on the National Qualifications Framework (NQF), promulgated in Government Gazette No.27819 of 20 July 2005; CAPS 3 MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 (iv) The policy document, An addendum to the policy document, the National Senior Certificate: A qualification at Level 4 on the National Qualifications Framework (NQF), regarding learners with special needs, published in Government Gazette, No.29466 of 11 December 2006, is incorporated in the…

4.grade_content: 3.3.1 Clarification of content for Grade 4 -- using the inverse relationship between addition and subtraction -- adding and subtracting units, multiples of 10 and multiples of 100 to/from any 3-digit number -- building up and breaking down numbers, -- rounding off to the nearest 10 and compensating -- dividing by 10, 100 and 1 000 -- multiplying by multiples 10 and 100 -- multiplying by 10 and 100 -- using multiplication to do division, -- doubling and halving, • Calculation techniques -- times tables involving multiplication of whole numbers to at least 10 x 10 ◊ multiples of 10 ◊ units -- number bonds: addition and subtraction facts for: • Number facts The mental Mathematics should systematically develop three aspects of learners’ number knowledge: Keep the number range lower in Term 1 and increase it during the year. At the start of the year, number ranges and calculations techniques can be based on those developed in Grade 3. The mental Mathematics programme should be developed systematically over the year. Learners should not be asked to do random calculations each day. As learners cover topics and develop calculating techniques in the main part of the lesson, so aspects of these can be incorporated into the mental Mathematics programme. Concepts and skills are developed through the main lesson, and then practised, with smaller number ranges in the mental Mathematics programme. SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES GRADE 4 TERM 1 10 minutes every day DURATION (in hours) MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 35 36 NUMBERS, OPERATIONS AND RELATIONSHIPS CONTENT AREA TOPICS Recognize and use the commutative; associative; and distributive properties of whole numbers Properties of whole numbers • counting beads • place value cards (flash cards) • a number grid Number range for multiples and factors Multiples of 1-digit numbers to at least 100 • a number line (structured and empty) Recommended apparatus Some mental Mathematics can be done without apparatus, but it is often useful to do mental Mathematics with apparatus. -- multiples -- odd and even numbers -- place value of up to 3-digit numbers -- ordering and comparing up to 3-digit numbers ◊ count forwards and backwards in 100s between 0 and at least 1 000 ◊ count forwards and backwards in 2s, 3s, 5s, 10s, 25s, 50s, between 0 and at least 500 -- counting: • Number concept SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES • using multiplication and division as inverse operations • using addition and subtraction as inverse operations • using a number line • doubling and halving • rounding off and compensating • building up and breaking down numbers • estimation Use a range of techniques to perform and check written and mental calculations of whole numbers including Calculation techniques CONCEPTS AND SKILLS GRADE 4 TERM 1 DURATION (in hours) MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) CAPS 1.1 NUMBERS, OPERATIONS AND RELATIONSHIPS counting, ordering, comparing, representing and place value of digits Whole numbers: TOPICS CONTENT AREA What is different to Grade 3? • Count forwards and backwards in 2s, 3s, 5s,10s 25s, 50s and 100s between 0 and at least 10 000 • Round off to the nearest 10, 100, 1 000 • Recognize the place value of digits in whole numbers to at least 4-digit numbers • Represent odd and even numbers to at least 1 000 • Order, compare and represent numbers to at least 4-digit numbers 2 hours In Term 1, learners should revise and consolidate work done in Grade 3. The list on the left is required by the end of the year. Recommended specifications are provided below. Number range for counting, ordering, comparing, representing and place value of digits 31 + 25 + 25 +25 + 25 -- other diagrams for counting e.g. -- arrays or diagrams of arrays e.g. + 25 -- pictures of objects, especially pictures of large numbers of objects that are presented in a grouped or structured way. An example of a picture of objects suitable for counting is provided at the end of the Grade 4 section of Numbers, Operations and Relationships. -- structured, semi-structured and empty number lines -- number grids -- counting beads -- counters • Counting should not only be thought of as verbal counting. Learners should count using apparatus such as • Count forwards and backwards in 2s, 3s, 5s, 10s, 25s, 50s, 100s between 0 and at least 1 000 Counting • Rounding off to the nearest DURATION (in hours) SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES CONCEPTS AND SKILLS GRADE 4 TERM 1 MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 37 38 1.1 NUMBERS, OPERATIONS AND RELATIONSHIPS counting, ordering, comparing, representing and place value of digits Whole numbers: TOPICS CONTENT AREA CONCEPTS AND SKILLS • All work developed here can be practised throughout the year in the mental Mathematics programme. -- Replace * with <,= or > Example: 89 * 98, 109 * 190 -- Indicate which of two numbers is greater or smaller e.g. 5 431 or 5 413 -- Show a given number on a structured or semi-structured number line, e.g. show which number is halfway between 340 and 350 on a number line ◊ on a number grid ◊ a sequence -- Fill in missing numbers in -- Arrange the given numbers below from the smallest to the biggest or biggest to smallest • Learners should be given a range of exercises such as: Compare and order (number range 0 to 999) • Recommended apparatus: place value/flash cards; Dienes blocks -- expanded notation -- place value or flash cards -- the number names (number words) • Learners should be able to break up numbers into hundreds, tens and units using Place value (number range 0 to 999) • Counting should not always start on the first multiple, nor should it always start on any other multiple e.g. counting in 2s can start from 5 or 27 or 348. SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES GRADE 4 TERM 1 DURATION (in hours) MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) CAPS -- substitution -- trial and improvement -- inspection At the start of the year learners can work with number sentences that help them to understand and learn about how to use the commutative and associative properties when calculating whole numbers. This will prepare them for the calculations that follow. The steps in any calculation are sets of equivalent statements. Exploring, understanding and learning the logic of the equivalent statements by working through patterns made up of number sentences, helps learners to learn calculating techniques. • The commutative, associative, and distributive properties of whole numbers and how we can use these properties to build up and break down numbers when we add and subtract • The inverse relationship between addition and subtraction -- multiples of 1 000 -- multiples of 100 -- multiples of 10 • Patterns in addition and subtraction number bonds for: In the Intermediate Phase it is useful to use number sentences as statements of equivalence. Patterns made up of number sentences will assist learners to make sense of and learn the following: Number sentences are also a way of showing equivalence. It seems obvious that what is written on the one side of the equal sign is equal to what is written on the other side. However but learners need to be trained to understand the equivalence. Examples of the above should be included at appropriate times throughout the year. Sometimes learners in the Intermediate Phase work with number sentences in isolation. However, it is more common for learners to work with number sentences and other forms of representation e.g. problems specified in words, numbers and calculations represented in flow diagrams. Number sentences can also be used as an equivalent form of expression to sections of flow diagram or tables. • Solve and complete number sentences by: (introduction to algebraic expressions) Writing number sentences can be seen as a way of preparing learners to write algebraic equations. Number sentences can be used to describe problem situations. Number sentences SOME…

5.grade_content: 3.3.2 Clarification of content for Grade 5 CAPS • Round off to the nearest 5, 10, 100 and 1 000 • Recognize the place value of digits in whole numbers to at least 4-digit numbers • Represent odd and even numbers to at least 1 000 • Order, compare and represent numbers to at least 4-digit numbers • Count forwards and backwards in whole number intervals up to at least 10 000 -- adding and subtracting of units, multiples of 10, 100, 1 000 to/from any 4-digit number -- rounding off to the nearest 10, 100 and 1 000 and compensating -- building up and breaking down numbers -- dividing by 10, 100 and 1 000, -- multiplying by multiples or 10, 100 and 1 000, -- multiplying by 10, 100 and 1 000 -- using multiplication to do division -- doubling and halving • calculation techniques -- times tables involving multiplication of whole numbers to at least 10x10 ◊ multiples of 10 Number range for counting, ordering, comparing, and representing numbers and for the place value of digits ◊ units -- units by multiples of 10 000 -- number bonds: addition and subtraction facts for: • number facts The mental Mathematics should systematically develop three aspects of learners’ number knowledge You can keep the number range lower in Term 1 and increase it during the year. At the start of the year, number ranges and calculations techniques can be based on those developed in Grade 4. The mental Mathematics programme should be developed systematically over the year. Learners should not be asked to do random calculations each day. As learners cover topics and develop calculating techniques in the main part of the lesson, so aspects of these can be incorporated into the mental Mathematics programme. Concepts and skills are developed through the main lesson, and then practised, sometimes with smaller number ranges in the mental Mathematics programme. 6.grade_content: 3.3.3 Clarification of content for Grade 6 10 minutes every day DURATION (in hours) MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 NUMBERS, OPERATIONS AND RELATIONSHIPS CONTENT AREA Mental Mathematics TOPICS 214 • 1 in terms of its multiplicative property • 0 in terms of its additive property • recognize and use the commutative; associative; distributive properties with whole numbers Properties of whole numbers • prime factors of numbers to at least 100 • factors of 2-digit and 3-digit whole numbers • counting beads • place value cards Number range for multiples and factors • multiples of 2-digit and 3-digit numbers • a number grid • a number line (structured, semi-structured or unstructured) Recommended apparatus Some mental Mathematics can be done without apparatus, but it is often useful to do mental Mathematics with apparatus -- multiples -- odd and even numbers -- building up and breaking down numbers -- place value SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES • using multiplication and division as inverse operations • using addition and subtraction as inverse operations • rounding off and compensating • building up and breaking down numbers • long division • adding, subtracting and multiplying in columns CONCEPTS AND SKILLS DURATION (in hours) MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) 1.1 NUMBERS, OPERATIONS AND RELATIONSHIPS CAPS Counting, ordering, representing and place value of digits Whole numbers TOPICS CONTENT AREA • Round off to the nearest 5, 10, 100 and 1 000 • Recognize the place value of digits in whole numbers to at least 9-digit numbers • Represent prime numbers to at least 100 Counting • Order, compare and represent numbers up to at least 9-digit numbers +9 +9 +9 +9 +9 +9 +9 215 Recommended apparatus: place value, flash cards, Dienes blocks • expanded notation • place value or flash cards • the number names (number words) Learners should be able to break up numbers into hundreds, tens and units using Place value(number range 0 to 999999) Counting should not always start with the first multiple. Nor should it always start on any other multiple e.g. counting in 25s can start from 27 or 113 , counting in 9’s can start from 2 641 or from 38 2461 • other diagrams for counting e.g. • arrays or diagrams of arrays e.g. • pictures of objects, especially pictures of large numbers of objects that are presented in a grouped or structured way. An example of a picture of objects suitable for counting is provided at the end of the Grade 5 section of Numbers, Operations and Relationships. • structured, semi-structured and empty number lines • number grids • counting beads • counters Counting should not only be thought of as verbal counting. Learners should count using apparatus such as Although counting in whole numbers is not specified in Grade 6, learners should be proficient in the Grade 5 level of counting. In Term 1, learners should revise and consolidate work done in Grade 5 SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES Number range for counting, ordering and representing, and place value of digits CONCEPTS AND SKILLS 2 hours DURATION (in hours) MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 NUMBERS, OPERATIONS AND RELATIONSHIPS CONTENT AREA 216 Counting, ordering, representing and place value of digits Whole numbers 1.1 TOPICS CONCEPTS AND SKILLS All work developed here can be practised throughout the year in the mental Mathematics programme. b) 784 109 £ 785 190 a) 247 889 £ 247 898 • Fill in <, = or > between the following: • Indicate which of two numbers is greater or smaller: 395431 or 395413? ◊ Show a given number on a number line – structured or semi-structured e.g. show on a number line which number is halfway between 471 340 and 471 350. -- on a number grid -- a sequence • Fill in missing numbers in • Arrange the given numbers below from the smallest to the biggest: or biggest to smallest Here learners should be given a range of exercises Compare and order SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES DURATION (in hours) MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) CAPS • Check solutions by substitution -- trial and improvement -- inspection • Solve and complete number sentences by (introduction to algebraic expressions) Number sentences • Write number sentences to describe problem situations 2.1 Patterns, Functions and Algebra CONCEPTS AND SKILLS Number sentences TOPICS CONTENT AREA • Completing number sentences with multiple operations • In Grade 6 learners do multiple operations with and without brackets. Learners can practise completing calculations in which the number sentence is written with brackets. This removes any confusion about the order of operations. Learners thus do not have to learn rules such as BODMAS if brackets are used routinely to indicate which operations have to be done first. • The steps in a calculation are sets of equivalent statements. Exploring, understanding and learning the logic of the equivalent statements by working through patterns made up of number sentences, helps learners to learn calculating techniques. -- dividing by 10, 100, 1 000 as this is useful for decimal fractions -- quick mental calculation techniques especially multiplying by multiples of 10, 100, 100, 10 000 -- the commutative, associative, and distributive properties with whole numbers and how we can use these properties together with building up and breaking down numbers when we calculate -- multiplication and division as inverse operations -- multiple operations with and without brackets and the order of operations • In Grade 6 it is useful to use number sentences, and patterns made up of number sentences to assist learners to make sense of and learn the following: • Number sentences are also a way of showing equivalence. It seems obvious that what is on the one side of the equal sign is equal to what is on the other side. However learners need to be trained to see that there are equivalent expressions on either side of the equals sign. • Sometimes in the Intermediate Phase learners work with number sentences in isolation from other work. However, it is more common for learners to work with number sentences together with other forms of representation e.g. problems specified in words, numbers and calculations represented in diagrams, flow diagrams. Examples are specified in appropriate places at different times of the year. • Number sentences can be used to describe problem situations. • Writing number sentences can be seen as a way of preparing learners to write algebraic equations. SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES 3 hours DURATION (in hours) MATHEMATICS GRADES 4-6 217 Patterns, Functions and Algebra CONTENT AREA 218 (introduction to algebraic expressions) Number sentences 2.1 TOPICS CONCEPTS AND SKILLS (23 – 7) x (8 – 4) (88 ÷ 4) – (88 ÷ 11) (79 – 21) ÷ 2 b) c) d) (20 + 5) x (20 + 7) 25(20 + 7) 20(20 + 7) + 5(20 + 7) b) c) d) (30 + 9) x (10 + 4) 14 (40 – 1) 10 (40 – 1) + 4(40 – 1) 30 (10 + 4) + 8 (10 + 4) b) c) d) e) CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) Learners can write a number sentence to show an array and then turn it through a right angle and write another multiplication number sentence to describe it. It is useful to confirm this by using arrays and number sentences. Example: 37 x 9 = 9 x 37 Numbers can be multiplied in any order. Commutative property of multiplication By Grade 6, learners should be familiar with the fact that you can add numbers in any order and that you can change the way you group numbers before adding them. Learners should know how to use the commutative and associative property of addition to simplify calculations. Using number sentences helps learners to consolidate the commutative and associative properties 39 x (10 x 4) a) 39 x 14 is equivalent to which of the following? Example 25 x (20 x 7) a) 25 x 27 is equivalent to which of the following? Example 12 ÷ (4 + 2) x 5 a) Examples SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES DURATION (in hours)…
